questions.description: Answer Part (a) [or]{.underline} Part (b) of Question One.
questions.text: Answer Part (a) [or]{.underline} Part (b) of Question One. Do [not]{.underline} answer both parts of this question. (a) What are the general principles of EU law and, using examples from the case-law of the CJEU, discuss how are they used by the CJEU to protect individuals and to ensure that the European Union institutions, and the Member States when implementing EU law, act in accordance with the rule of law? [OR]{.underline} (b) In Case C-235/17, Commission v Hungary ECLI:EU:C:2019:423, the CJEU reminded us that:- [...] it should be borne in mind that the fundamental rights guaranteed by the Charter are applicable in all situations governed by EU law and that they must, therefore, be complied with inter alia where national legislation falls within the scope of EU law. [para. 63] In this Judgment, the CJEU was addressing issues related to Article 17 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union on the right to property. With reference to the case-law of the CJEU throughout your answer, assess how the CJEU has allowed the Charter of Fundamental Rights to be used to challenge a wide range of EU and national legislative measures in the manner described in Commission v Hungary (above).

questions.description: Outline, by reference to relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case-law of the Union judicature, the…
questions.text: Outline, by reference to relevant TEU and TFEU Treaty provisions and pertinent case-law of the Union judicature, the roles of (i) the European Parliament; (ii) the Commission; and (iii) the Council in establishing new or amending legislative, delegated and implementing acts. Assess also whether the changes made to legislative procedures by the Lisbon Treaty amendments have improved the democratic legitimacy of decision-making within the European Union.

questions.description: The EU Commission introduced (fictional) Directive (EU) No 2020/2020 on 29… Directive, Regulation.
questions.text: The EU Commission introduced (fictional) Directive (EU) No 2020/2020 on 29 February 2020, setting a series of harmonising provisions on the use and disposal of single-use plastic. Article 12 of the directive set the date for implementation as 1 April 2021. Article 4 of the EU Directive provides that certain products should no longer be made with single use plastic. This list is currently limited to plastic forks, plastic knives and plastic straws (but can be added to using delegated legislation). Producers could continue to sell off any single use plastic products that they had already produced by 1 April 2021, but they were no longer permitted to make these products after this date. Article 5 of the Directive provides that single use plastic products should never be disposed by incineration due to the potential release of toxic fumes from this. The (fictional) European Union (Single Use Plastics) Regulations 2021, SI No. 987/2021, were introduced on 30 November 2021 to transpose EU Directive 2020/2020 into Irish Law. They came into effect on the date of publication. Portmarnock Plastics Ltd. have been producing plastic products in Dublin since 2005, including plastic cutlery, both knives and forks. As they knew that they would no longer be able sell these products in Ireland under the terms of the Directive and the Irish Regulations, they switched to only using recyclable plastic in their products by the end of March 2021. Recyclable plastic is more expensive to use in production. Portlaoise Plastics plc continued to manufacture single-use plastic knives and forks right up until the end of November 2021 and have since been continuing to sell off this remaining stock that they produced. Portmarnock Plastics Ltd lost out on a lucrative contract to supply disposable cutlery to a large Irish supermarket chain in late December 2021. They had a contract to supply these knives and forks to the supermar ket chain on an ongoing basis since soon after they went into business in 2005. However, on this occasion they were not able to compete on price with what Portlaoise Plastics were charging for their single-use stock as Portmarnock Plastics had sold off the last of their single-use stock by August 2021. Roger is a resident of Ringsend. He has chronic asthma, which he claims has been made worse by the toxic fumes emitted from the incinerator beside his home. The incinerator is still used in March 2022 for the burning of single use plastics. The (fictional) European Union (Single Use Plastics) Regulations 2021 contained no provision reflecting what was set out in Article 5 of EU Directive 2020/2020 prohibiting this practice. Referring to CJEU case-law throughout, advise both (i) Portmarnock Plastics Ltd.; and (ii) Roger on any remedies that they may have under EU law arising out of the way that EU Directive 2020/2020 has been transposed into Irish Law.

questions.description: Has the seminal decision of the CJEU in Case 25/62 Plaumann v Commission continued to make it 'literally impossible'…
questions.text: Has the seminal decision of the CJEU in Case 25/62 Plaumann v Commission continued to make it 'literally impossible' for a non-privileged applicant to 'institute proceedings against an act' of the European Union under Article 263(4) TFEU? In your opinion, refer to relevant case-law, legislation and to attempts that may have been made (particularly by the amendment introduced by the Treaty of Lisbon), to make it easier for non- privileged applicants to use Article 263 TFEU to review the legality of acts of the EU institutions. Consider also how any such attempts have been interpreted by the Union judicature in its relevant case-law.

questions.description: Following a series of scandals surrounding the contamination of powdered… Directive.
questions.text: Following a series of scandals surrounding the contamination of powdered baby milk and powdered infant formula produced in France, China and Brazil, the Irish Government, through the Department of Health, wants to establish a (fictional) scheme for the sampling and analysing of all such products, regardless of whether they are manufactured in Ireland, in other EU Member States, or in any third country. The (fictional) EU Directive 2017/678 on powdered baby milk and follow-on infant formula allows EU Member States to carry out sampling and analysis of these products, but it does not oblige any Member State to do so. According to the Irish Government's proposal, the costs of operating this scheme will be met by the imposition of a 10% value added taxon all imported powdered baby milk and powdered infant formula. The products of Irish producers will not be taxed in this way, but will, instead, have to be certified regarding the sampling and analysis undertaken and detailing the results of these investigations. Iris producers will have to pay for these tests and the related certification themselves. It is estimated that this will add an additional production cost to the Irish producers of powdered baby milk and powdered infant formula of between 8-12%. You have been approached to advise the Irish Department of Health about the compatibility of the proposed scheme with EU law. In your opinion, referring to relevant case law of the CJEU and having regard to the (fictional) EU Directive 2017/678, consider the compatibility of the proposed scheme with the TFEU provisions on charges of equivalent effect to customs duties and internal taxation.

questions.description: Betty is a 77 year old woman who has lived for all of her life in… Advise Betty on whether she may still be able to…
questions.text: Betty is a 77 year old woman who has lived for all of her life in Belmullet, County Mayo. She represented the county camogie team for eleven years, from 1967-1978, when injuries to her right leg and lower back forced her to retire from the sport. Caused in part by a resultant limp when she walks, Betty developed problems with her left hip in the early 2000s. This has got progressively worse. When she visited her GP about the pain in 2008, she (the GP) referred Betty on to an orthopaedic consultant who told her that she would eventually need a hip replacement operation, but probably not for a few more years. The pain and discomfort in Betty's left hip continued to get worse, so much so that she has required the support of a walking stick since 2012.She visited a different orthopaedic consultant in 2016, who indicated that due to the high level of discomfort and deterioration she would need to have the surgery as soon as possible. It was later scheduled to take place in November 2019, but Betty had to postpone the surgery at that time due do a bad case of influenza. It was rescheduled for March 2020, but was cancelled by the public hospital due to the suspension of all elective surgeries at that time due to the covid pandemic. By January 2022, and despite numerous enquiries made by Betty, the hip replacement operation had still not been rescheduled. She was in a considerable amount of discomfort and could not walk for more than 50 meters at a time, even with a walking stick. She found that both her discomfort and her lack of mobility had taken a toll on her mental health as well. She became quite reclusive and her appetite waned. Her daughter advised her to travel to Germany to a private hospital to have the hip replacement operation there, which she did at the start of February 2022. The German hospital charged Betty €21,500 for the operation and 7 nights residence in a semi-private room in the hospital. She also paid €1,200 for an additional five nights in a local hotel in Germany as she was advised not to travel back to Ireland for an additional week after her hospital discharge. The return flights to Germany cost €225. With subsistence and other travel costs €1,100 per month and her life savings amount to €21,000. Due to the pressure that this operation has put on her financial situation, Betty has approached you for advice on whether there is any provision in EU law that would enable her to recoup some of this money from the Irish Health Service Executive (HSE). Under the (fictional) HSE Treatment (EU) Scheme, reimbursement for medical procedures will only be available for public patients where they have first had an application approved under the scheme (at least 9 months prior to travel for the operation) and up to a maximum payment of €9,000. Betty made no application to the HSE for this type of support before the operation because she was unaware of the scheme's existence. The standard cost across the EU for a hip operation replacement and hospital recovery is €22,000. Referring to relevant CJEU case-law and EU legislation throughout, advise Betty on whether she may still be able to make a claim for reimbursement for her operation from the HSE and on any potential difficulties that she may encounter in making this claim.

questions.description: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the…
questions.text: Write a case-note on any THREE of the following eight judgments of the CJEU, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the CJEU, and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgment: (i) Cases T-778/16, Ireland v Commission, and T-892/16, Apple Sales International and Apple Operations Europe V Commission ECLI:EU:T:2020:338; (ii) Case 120/78, Rewe-Zentral AG v Bundermonopolverwalting fur Branntwein (Cassis de Dijon) [1979] ECR 649; (iii) Case C-341/05, Laval un Partneri Ltd v Svenska Byggnadsarbetareforbundet ea [2007] ECR I-11767; (iv) Case C-212/97, Centros Ltd v Erhvervs-og Selskabsstyrelsen [1999] ECR 1459; (v) Cases 41, 44 and 45/69, ACF Chemiefarma NV v Commission (Quinine cartel) [1970] ECR 661; (vi) Case C-236/09, Association Belge des Consommateurs Test-Achats ASBL and others v Conseil des ministres[2011]ECRI-773; (vii) Case C-378/17, Minister for Justice and Equality and the the Garda Siochana v Workplace Relations Commission ECLI:EU:C:2018:979; (viii) Case 174/82, Officier van Justitie v Sandoz BV [1983] ECR 2445.

questions.description: With reference to relevant case-law and Commission decisions, analyse extent to which Article 106 TFEU provides for an…
questions.text: With reference to relevant case-law and Commission decisions, analyse extent to which Article 106 TFEU provides for an exception to the competition law, particularly Articles 101 and 102 TFEU, and the scope and such exception as regards public undertakings, undertakings to which have granted special or exclusive rights and undertakings entrusted by with the operation of services of general economic interest.

questions.description: In Case C-235/17, Commission v Hungary ECLl:EU:C:2019:423, the CJEU reminded us that: [...
questions.text: In Case C-235/17, Commission v Hungary ECLl:EU:C:2019:423, the CJEU reminded us that: [... ] it should be borne in mind that the fundamental rights guaranteed by the Charter are applicable in all situations governed by EU law and that they must, therefore, be complied with inter alia where national legislation falls within the scope of EU law. [para. 63] In this Judgment, the CJEU was addressing issues related to Article 17 of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union on the right to property. With reference to the case law of the CJEU concerning the Charter throughout your answer, assess how the CJEU in that case law, since the Charter was declared in December 2000, but especially since it came into force in December 2009 along with the Treaty of Lisbon, allowed provisions of the Charter to be used to challenge a wide range of both EU and national legislative measures in the manner described in Commission v Hungary (above).

questions.description: Has the introduction of the ordinary legislative procedure under Article 289 TFEU set the type of role for the European…
questions.text: Has the introduction of the ordinary legislative procedure under Article 289 TFEU set the type of role for the European Parliament in the legislative process that should satisfactorily address any outstanding concerns that may have existed before the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon about a lack of democratic decision-making in the creation of European Union laws?

questions.description: Fictitious Directive 2020/777 of the European Parliament and of the… Directive, Mortgage.
questions.text: Fictitious Directive 2020/777 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 30 June 2020 on the harmonisation of gambling services includes amongst its main provisions: Article 3, which requires setting a total maximum betting daily limit of €500 per person, applicable in all EU Member States for both in-shop and online gambling; and Article 4, which obliges EU Member States to charge of levy of 10% on all individual bets over €200 (as a form of deterrent on gamblers placing excessive stakes). These provisions were to be transposed into the national laws of all EU Member States by 31st December 2021. The Irish Government have amended several pieces of domestic legislation, including the (fictitious) Gaming and Lotteries Act 1986, the Gambling Control Act 2012 and the Betting Act 1990 to reflect the new requirements set by Fictitious Directive 2020/777. However, given the complexity of current Irish law on gambling, none of these amendments will come into effect until January 31st 2023. On May 12th 2022 Billy went into his local privately-owned betting shop and placed three separate bets of €300 each on three greyhound races. He also staked another €225 with a French-registered online gambling website on the outcome of a football match. He won on none of these bets. Over the following week he gambled another €4,500 to try and recoup his losses. Only one bet came good, giving him winnings of €250 and a net loss for that week of €4,250. Billy's gambling in May 2022 has used up all of his savings and placed him in debt. He cannot now afford to pay the mortgage on his home and is threatened with house repossession. Billy has just found out about the existence of Directive 2020/777 on the harmonisation of gambling services. He comes to you as his solicitor for advice on whether he may have any grounds under EU law for taking an action against either the betting shops and/or the Irish Government. Referring to relevant CJEU case law throughout, advise Billy.

questions.description: When Advocate General Jacobs stated in paragraph 103 of his Opinion on…
questions.text: When Advocate General Jacobs stated in paragraph 103 of his Opinion on Case C- 50/2000, UPA v Council, that: "[. ..] an individual should be regarded as individually concerned within the meaning of the fourth paragraph of Article 230 EC [now Article 263 TFEU] by a Community measure where, by reason of his particular circumstances, the measure has, or is liable to have, a substantial adverse effect on his interests" He was clearly articulating that there was at that time a lack of effective judicial protection for non-privileged applicants who sought a judicial review of acts of the European Union's institutions. Firstly, explain briefly, by reference to relevant contemporaneous case-law, the concerns that the Advocate General was addressing here. Secondly, make an assessment of whether these concerns have been adequately addressed by the changes that were made to Article 230 EC by what is now contained in Article 263 TFEU and relevant case-law of the Union judicature interpreting those changes.

questions.description: Answer Part (A) [OR]{.underline} Part (B). Advise both Sudds Ltd.
questions.text: Answer Part (A) [OR]{.underline} Part (B). Do not attempt both parts. Part (A) The Irish Minister of the Environment, Climate and Communications has launched a public consultation as part of the process of introducing new, and updating existing, national measures designed to reduce the consumption of fossil fuels in Ireland. Amongst these are proposals to: - (i) introduce an additional charge of €0.50 per kilogram of soap produced from petroleum but reduce charges per kilogram of soap produced from rapeseed oil from €0.40 to €0.05; and (ii) prohibit the use of domestic electric car chargers where the electricity used to charge these cars is produced from fossil fuels. You have been approached by two companies - Sudds Ltd., an Irish manufacturer of soaps which currently uses petroleum imported from other EU Member States to make its products (rapeseed oil is widely manufactured in Ireland whereas petroleum is not); and ltalycity S.p.A., who manufacture domestic electric car chargers and fear that their sales in Ireland may be detrimentally impacted if the proposals come to fruition. Referring to relevant case law of the CJEU throughout, advise both Sudds Ltd. and ltalycity S.p.A. on the compatibility of these proposals with EU law on the free movement of goods. Part (B) Write a legal opinion, addressing relevant Treaty provisions and CJEU case-law, on whether the introduction by the Irish Government of an additional €2 tax on the sale of every bottle of wine from off-licences and restaurants may be contrary to Article 110 TFEU. You may presume that Ireland produces very little wine when compared to other alcoholic beverages that are not subject to the new additional €2 specific tax.

questions.description: Caroline is a French national who moved to Ireland in March 2016 to join a… Advise Caroline on whether she may be…
questions.text: Caroline is a French national who moved to Ireland in March 2016 to join a commune based in Connemara. Caroline resided with the commune from March 2016, where she was provided with a room and board in return for making pottery in the workshop. All pottery was sold to the public and the revenue generated from this was used to finance the activities of the commune. In May 2022, Caroline fell out with one of her fellow commune potters who had accused her of stealing some pottery that she had made and later deliberately breaking it. Caroline denied the accusations and felt so aggrieved at the lack of support from her fellow commune residents that she felt that she had to leave the commune, even though she had nowhere else to go. After sleeping on the streets of Clifden for the first few nights after leaving the commune, Caroline obtained some casual work on a fruit farm where she was paid according to the amount of fruit that she picked. On an average day, Caroline would be paid €35 - €40 for eight hours of work. The minimum wage in Ireland is €10.50 per hour and the living wage is estimated to be €12.17 per hour in 2022. During this time, Caroline started squatting in the holiday home of a partner in a large Dublin law firm who, when he found out and saw the extensive and costly damage that she had done to his house, reported it to An Garcia Siochana. Caroline was charged and convicted of criminal damage, being sentenced to eighteen months in prison in July 2022. As Caroline's solicitor, you have been advised that the Department of Justice is seeking to deport Caroline back to France as soon as her sentence is served. Caroline does not want to go back to France but now wants to return to the commune in Connemara if they accept that she did not deliberately steal or break any other potter's work. Referring to relevant Treaty provisions, legislation and CJEU case-law throughout, advise Caroline on whether she may be protected from deportation under EU law.

questions.description: Write a case-note on any [THREE]{.underline} of the following eight judgments of the Union judicature, setting out in…
questions.text: Write a case-note on any [THREE]{.underline} of the following eight judgments of the Union judicature, setting out in each case-note: (a) the core facts; (b) the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration by the Union judicature, and (c) how those issues were addressed by the CJEU in its judgement: (i) Case C-34/09, Gerardo Ruiz Zambrano v ONEm [2011] ECR 1-1177; (ii) Case 33/74, JHM van Binsbergen v Bestuur van de Bedrijfsvereniging voor de Metaalnijverheid [1974] ECR 1299; (iii) Cases T-778/16, Ireland v Commission, and T-892/16, Apple Sales International and Apple Operations Europe v Commission ECLl:EU:T:2020:338; (iv) Case 87/75, CO Bresciani v Amministrazione ltaliana de/le Finanze [1976] ECR 129; (v) Case C-236/09, Association Beige des Consommateurs Test-Achats ASBL and others v Conseil des ministres [2011] ECR 1-773; (vi) Case 6/64, Flaminio Costa v ENEL [1964] ECR 585; (vii) Case C-415/93, Union Royale Beige des Societes de Football Association ASBL v Jean-Marc Bosman [1995] ECR 1-4921; (viii) Case C-378/17, Minister for Justice and Equality and the Commissioner of the Garda Siochana v Workplace Relations Commission ECLl:EU:C:2018:979;

questions.description: Explain, by reference to relevant case-law of the Union judicature, the concepts of "agreement", "decision" and…
questions.text: Explain, by reference to relevant case-law of the Union judicature, the concepts of "agreement", "decision" and "concerted practice" in Article 101(1) TFEU. In addition, explain separately, by reference to relevant case-law, how a "dominant position" can be held by "one or more undettakings" under Article 102 TFEU.
